mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Freelander 2.5 V6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 2.5 V6 engine typically costs £1,800–£2,800 supply only. Used engines are available from £500–£1,200. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available — the KV6 has been out of production since 2006.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Freelander 2.5 V6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.5 KV6 is smooth and refined with a lovely V6 soundtrack, but it has a well-earned reputation for head gasket issues, thermostat housing leaks, and cambelt failures. The upper inlet manifold gasket ('V' gasket) is the most notorious weak point — it leaks coolant into the V of the engine, causing overheating. Regular maintenance and preventative replacement of the V-gasket and cambelt are essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Freelander 2.5 V6?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are upper inlet manifold gasket failure ('V' gasket — coolant leaks into the V of the engine), thermostat housing leaks, and cambelt tensioner wear. The engine is an interference design — if the cambelt snaps, pistons and valves collide, destroying the engine.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Freelander 2.5 V6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £3,000 Freelander, a £2,300 recon is 77% of the car's value — exceeding our 60% Land Rover threshold for petrol engines. Against a tired £1,500 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold significantly — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained 2.5 V6 Freelanders are becoming rare, however.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Freelander 2.5 V6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 2.5 V6 carries the engine code KV6 (also referred to as 25K4F in Rover parts documentation). It's a 2.5-litre naturally aspirated V6 petrol engine used in the Freelander 1 (FL1) from 2000 to 2006.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the head gasket issues, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends. If caught early (the upper inlet manifold gasket), the fix is £600–£1,200 — well worth doing. If ignored and the engine has overheated severely, the head gasket itself may have failed, requiring an £800–£1,800 head gasket replacement. If the engine has overheated to the point of warped heads or cracked liners, a full engine replacement (£1,800–£3,200) is often more cost-effective. Prevention is the key — replace the V-gasket preventatively every 60,000 miles or whenever the cambelt is done.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Freelander 2.5 V6 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from Land Rover

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the Rover KV6 2.5-litre V6 petrol engine. Used engines are available from approximately £500–£1,200, with reconditioned units ranging from £1,800–£2,800. Main dealer new engines are no longer available — the KV6 has been out of production since 2006.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Freelander 2.5 V6 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare 2.5 V6 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Head Gasket Failure (Upper Inlet Manifold Gasket)
problems.symptoms: Overheating, coolant loss, white smoke from exhaust, mayonnaise-like sludge in oil filler cap, engine running rough.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,200 (upper manifold gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: The KV6's "V" gasket (the upper inlet manifold gasket) is the single most notorious failure point on this engine. It leaks coolant into the V of the engine, causing overheating and eventually head gasket failure if ignored. The fix is well-understood — replace the upper manifold gasket and the thermostat housing at the same time, as they often fail together. Do not delay if you see coolant loss or overheating.

failureLink.label: Read the full head gasket failure guide

problems.title: Thermostat Housing & Cooling System Leaks
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, overheating, sweet smell from the engine bay, heater not working properly.
problems.recommendation: The KV6's plastic thermostat housing is another common weak point. It becomes brittle with age and can crack, leading to coolant loss. When it fails, the engine can overheat quickly, potentially leading to the head gasket failure described above. If you're experiencing unexplained coolant loss, have the thermostat housing and cooling system inspected promptly.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: Cambelt Tensioner & Belt Failure
problems.symptoms: Rattling or squealing from the front of the engine, cambelt warning light (if fitted), sudden loss of power.
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£3,200 (if belt snaps — engine is an interference design)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear unusual noises from the belt area, stop driving

problems.recommendation: The KV6 is an interference engine — if the cambelt snaps, pistons and valves collide, resulting in catastrophic engine damage. Land Rover recommended cambelt replacement every 60,000 miles or 5 years. Many examples have not had this done, making them time bombs. If buying a 2.5 V6 Freelander, confirm cambelt history — and if it's due or unknown, budget £300–£600 to get it done immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full cambelt failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Freelander 2.5 V6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Well-understood fix — caught early, engine survives
Preventative — far cheaper than a snapped belt
Labour-intensive; often more than the vehicle is worth
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 2.5 V6? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented upper inlet manifold gasket replacement (or "V" gasket done)
* Cambelt and tensioner replaced within the last 40,000 miles or 4 years
* Cooling system service records (thermostat housing, water pump replaced)
* Full service history with evidence of coolant changes and regular maintenance
avoidIf: * No proof of cambelt replacement within the last 60,000 miles or 5 years
* Unexplained coolant loss with no cooling system service records
* Mayonnaise-like sludge in the oil filler cap (head gasket failure warning)
* Overheating history with no documented repair — walk away

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 2.5 V6 is the most desirable petrol engine in the Freelander 1 — smooth, refined, and with a lovely V6 soundtrack. But it comes with a well-earned reputation for head gasket failures, thermostat housing leaks, and cambelt issues. These are not ifs — they're whens. Buy only with documented history of the "V" gasket replacement and cambelt service. A sorted 2.5 V6 Freelander is a £1,500–£3,000 classic; a neglected one is a £2,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare 2.5 V6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Freelander 2.5 V6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or thermostat housing replacement? (KV6's Achilles heel)
* Oil Filler Cap — Check for mayonnaise-like sludge (head gasket failure warning)
* Cambelt History — Has the cambelt and tensioner been replaced within the last 60,000 miles or 5 years? (essential)
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or squealing from the cambelt area
* Overheating — Any history of overheating? (often linked to V-gasket or thermostat housing failure)
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate coolant ingress
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved cooling system or misfire issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (cats, O2 sensors)
